stories.headline: Update: E-bike rider who died on I-70 was fleeing police, Denver authorities say
stories.summary:
An e-bike cyclist who died last week after a crash in Denver's Elyria Swansea neighborhood was allegedly attempting to evade officers when the fatal collision occurred, Denver police revealed Sunday. Officers had tried to stop the cyclist for hazardous traffic violations before the rider entered eastbound Interstate 70 in an apparent attempt to flee, according to Officer Siena Riley.

The cyclist crashed with a vehicle near Steele Street and eastbound I-70 just before 7:30 p.m. Thursday and was transported to a hospital, where they were pronounced dead. The cyclist has not been publicly identified.

The crash is now under internal investigation by the Denver Police Department. The disclosure that officers were pursuing the cyclist at the time of the incident adds a significant new dimension to the case, which had initially been reported as a straightforward traffic fatality.

stories.headline: Man dies while in custody at Denver's Downtown Detention Center
stories.summary:
A man was found unresponsive in a housing unit at Denver's Downtown Detention Center on Sunday afternoon and later pronounced dead, the Denver Sheriff Department announced. The man, who has not been identified, was discovered at approximately 2 p.m. and transported to Denver Health Medical Center, where he died.

The Denver Police Department is conducting an investigation into the circumstances of the death. The Sheriff's Department did not release details about what led to the man's incarceration or any preliminary findings.

In-custody deaths at the Downtown Detention Center have drawn scrutiny in recent years over conditions and medical care at the facility. No further information was immediately available.

stories.headline: Aurora police investigate 3 separate overnight shootings over the weekend
stories.summary:
Aurora police are investigating three distinct shooting incidents that occurred over the course of one weekend, leaving one person with life-threatening injuries and two others wounded. The first incident, shortly after midnight Saturday, involved a juvenile who arrived at a hospital with a non-life-threatening gunshot wound; investigators located shell casings and believe an exchange of gunfire occurred in the 1400 block of North Dayton Street.

Two more shootings took place in the early hours of Sunday morning. Just before 1 a.m., an adult woman was struck by gunfire in the 14000 block of East Iowa Drive while in the area; she was treated and released. About 20 minutes later, an adult man suffered life-threatening injuries in a shooting near a large gathering in the 11600 block of East 16th Avenue.

No suspects have been identified or arrested in any of the three cases. All investigations remain active, and Aurora police are asking anyone with information to come forward.

stories.headline: Man rescued in Wheat Ridge trench after being trapped nearly 24 hours
stories.summary:
A man was rescued Saturday night after spending nearly a full day trapped at the bottom of a 15-foot-deep trench in a Wheat Ridge construction zone, according to authorities. A passerby discovered the man near North Crossing Drive and Clear Creek Drive and contacted emergency services; West Metro Fire Rescue crews arrived at approximately 9:30 p.m. and found him lying in mud and water in the narrow hole.

Two firefighters descended into the trench to assess the man's condition and prepare him for extraction. He was secured in a basket and lifted to the surface using a rope system before being transported to a hospital.

The man's injuries were not considered life-threatening, officials said. It remains unclear how he came to fall into the trench or why it took nearly 24 hours for anyone to discover him.

stories.headline: Gross Dam's $600 million expansion is complete, but Denver Water faces legal fight over filling new reservoir
stories.summary:
Denver Water's Gross Dam expansion — now Colorado's tallest dam at 470 feet — reached a major construction milestone on June 3, when workers placed the final layer of roller-compacted concrete on the structure. The $600 million project, 12 years in the making, raises the dam 131 feet and triples Gross Reservoir's water storage capacity, a goal Denver Water has pursued for more than two decades as a hedge against drought and population growth.

Despite the engineering achievement, it remains uncertain whether Denver Water will ever be permitted to fill the reservoir to its new capacity. A federal judge ruled in 2024 that regulators violated environmental protection laws by failing to adequately analyze the project's impact on the broader Colorado River Basin and by not considering less harmful alternatives; she subsequently barred the utility from filling the expanded reservoir and ordered the permitting process to be redone.

Denver Water is now asking the 10th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals to reverse that ruling, with a three-judge panel scheduled to hear arguments on July 31 in Santa Fe. Environmental groups, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and multiple Front Range water providers have all filed briefs, signaling the case's broad implications for Western water law and infrastructure.

stories.headline: Denver forecast: Near-99-degree heat and critical fire weather conditions expected by midweek
stories.summary:
After a cool and cloudy Sunday with a high near 68 degrees and afternoon thunderstorm chances, Denver is expected to heat up sharply through the week, according to the National Weather Service in Boulder. Temperatures are forecast to approach 99 degrees by Wednesday, a dramatic swing of more than 30 degrees in just a few days.

Wednesday is also expected to be breezy, with the combination of extreme heat and gusty winds creating conditions the National Weather Service has characterized as potentially 'critical fire weather.' The forecast is particularly significant given that six wildfires are already burning across Colorado as fire season intensifies statewide.

Residents are advised to monitor updated forecasts as conditions evolve. The return of intense summer heat follows a weekend that brought temporary relief from earlier high temperatures across the metro area.

stories.headline: Broncos' Burnham Yard stadium district vision faces major hurdles despite $186 million in land acquisitions
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have now been tied to at least $186 million in land purchases surrounding the Burnham Yard railyard in Denver, where the organization is pursuing a new stadium district targeting a 2031 opening. The Broncos envision a mixed-use development anchored by a new stadium, following a national trend toward stadium districts that create self-contained mini-neighborhoods with retail, residential, and entertainment uses.

Small property owners and developers already active in the area say the Broncos' timeline faces significant obstacles. Sean Herman, who bought a nearby parcel in 2023, said a simple building remodel took him a year and a half to navigate through city processes — and that was before contending with repeated break-ins, theft of copper wire, and other property damage. 'I'm so skeptical that they're going to pull it off in time,' Herman said.

Beyond individual property challenges, the Broncos must also contend with city permitting timelines, community relations with existing landowners, and the broader complexity of coordinating a large-scale urban development project. The team has not finalized a purchase agreement for the railyard itself, and the surrounding area still contains numerous independent businesses and developers with their own competing visions for the site.

stories.headline: Davis Webb steps into Broncos offensive coordinator role with head coaching future already a topic of discussion
stories.summary:
Davis Webb took the field for his first public sessions as the Denver Broncos' new offensive coordinator this week, but conversation quickly turned to where his rapidly ascending career might take him next. Webb, 31, has moved from backup quarterback to quarterbacks coach to passing game coordinator to play-caller in just four years, and nearly landed NFL head coaching jobs in Buffalo and Las Vegas this past winter.

Any near-term speculation about Webb eventually succeeding Sean Payton in Denver was largely put to rest when Payton signed a new five-year contract extension Thursday. Payton, 62, said he has 'a lot of juice left,' effectively extending his Denver tenure deep into the next decade and beyond Webb's likely horizon as an offensive coordinator.

Colleagues and players within the Broncos organization speak in superlatives about Webb's football intellect, with words like 'genius' and 'savant' used in informal conversation around the facility. Webb himself said he is focused on his new role and the opportunity to call plays alongside quarterback Bo Nix, though those close to the situation widely expect his head coaching opportunity will come sooner rather than later.

stories.headline: Avalanche GM Joe Sakic says Cale Makar extension is top priority, expects star defenseman to finish career in Colorado
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche general manager Joe Sakic said publicly that extending defenseman Cale Makar is the organization's top offseason priority, declaring that Makar is 'going to finish his career' in Colorado. Makar, widely considered the best defenseman in the NHL, is approaching the end of his current contract and has drawn widespread speculation about a potential new deal.

Sakic's comments signal the Avalanche's firm intention to retain the 25-year-old Alberta native, who won the Conn Smythe Trophy as playoff MVP during Colorado's 2022 Stanley Cup championship run and has been a Norris Trophy finalist in multiple seasons. Any extension is expected to set a new benchmark for defenseman salaries in the league.

The announcement comes as the Avalanche face a busy offseason following their playoff exit. Locking up Makar long-term would provide a cornerstone around which the team can continue to build.

stories.headline: Colorado hot air balloon operator in Winter Park named best in the United States by USA Today
stories.summary:
Grand Adventure Balloon Tours, based in Winter Park, Colorado, has been ranked the top hot air balloon ride in the United States by USA Today's 10Best readers' choice awards program. The company offers year-round flights over Colorado scenery, with views of the Continental Divide and Rocky Mountain National Park, and occasionally provides passengers the chance to fly low enough to spot wildlife or participate in a 'splash and dash' over a nearby pond or river.

USA Today 10Best selects nominees through an expert panel and then opens voting to readers to determine final rankings. The recognition highlights Winter Park and the broader Colorado mountain corridor as a premier destination for adventure tourism.

The award is likely to draw increased visitor interest to Grand Adventure Balloon Tours and the Winter Park area heading into the summer tourism season.

stories.headline: Federal oil and gas lease sale proposed for 127,000 acres in Colorado and eight other Western states
stories.summary:
A proposed federal sale of oil and gas leases covering 127,000 acres across nine Western states, including Colorado, is moving forward as a result of federal legislation requiring quarterly lease sales in the region. The sale represents a significant volume of public land that could be opened to energy development across the West.

The proposal comes amid ongoing debate in Colorado over the balance between energy production, environmental protection, and the state's climate commitments. Colorado has in recent years enacted stricter regulations governing oil and gas development on state lands, though federal lease decisions are controlled by the Bureau of Land Management.

Environmental groups and energy industry representatives are expected to weigh in as the proposed lease sale moves through the federal process. The acreage and specific Colorado parcels involved were not detailed in initial reports.

joke.joke: People who read the tabloids deserve to be lied to.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Announce Ceasefire Deal, Strait of Hormuz to Reopen Under Agreement
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran have agreed to an immediate and permanent cessation of hostilities, President Donald Trump announced Sunday on Truth Social, declaring the Strait of Hormuz open to toll-free shipping and authorizing the removal of a U.S. naval blockade. Iran's Supreme National Security Council confirmed the agreement via state news outlet Tasnim, saying all warfare between the parties would cease "immediately and permanently from tonight." Iran's deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi also confirmed the deal publicly.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced the agreement on X minutes before Trump's statement, crediting mediation efforts by Pakistan, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey. A formal signing ceremony is scheduled for Friday, June 19, in Switzerland, with Sharif indicating "pre-implementation discussions" would take place in the interim. Trump indicated the Strait would open officially upon the Friday signing, in part to allow for mine removal operations, though the U.S. military has not confirmed that Iran placed mines in the waterway.

The conflict began February 28, when the United States and Israel launched coordinated strikes across Iran, including the assassination of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. A ceasefire had previously been reached but fighting continued on multiple fronts, including in Lebanon. Trump said in a separate post that the deal would bring "peace and security to the whole region" and suggested a more comprehensive agreement would follow, adding in a New York Times interview that the U.S. could resume military operations or take on a broader regional security role in exchange for revenues if terms were not honored.

Israel's position adds uncertainty to the agreement's durability. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said Monday that Israeli forces would remain indefinitely in territory seized in Lebanon, Gaza, and Syria, and warned that any Iranian strike on Israel in response to ongoing Israeli operations would draw retaliation. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu was seeking a meeting with Trump, possibly in Washington, to discuss the deal, according to an Israeli official briefed on the matter. The Strait of Hormuz, through which a significant share of global oil shipments pass, has been largely closed since the conflict began in late February.
